title: Supporting Someone After Stroke
subtitle: Communication Strategies That Help
description: Practical communication strategies based on caregiver experience
about: This document was created based on personal experience supporting a family member after a stroke, combined with general recovery research. Every stroke is different. These ideas are simply practical strategies that may help reduce frustration and improve daily interaction.
disclaimer: This guide is not medical advice. Stroke recovery varies greatly from person to person. Always follow guidance from healthcare professionals. Use what is helpful and ignore what is not.
why: After a stroke, the brain systems responsible for motivation, initiation, and focus may be weakened. What can look like stubbornness or lack of effort is often neurological.
The brain may need more time to start tasks, process requests, or shift attention. This is the result of injury, not personality.
Too much pressure can cause the brain to shut down.
A calm and simple approach helps it re-engage.

principle.title: Core Principle
lines: Start with calm support, then invite action.

principleBridge: The strategies below are simple ways to apply this approach in everyday interactions.

strategies.title: Use Simple Language
strategies.instead: You really should get up and walk a little bit. It would be good for you.
strategies.note: Small steps are easier for the brain to start.

strategies.title: Offer Two Choices
strategies.instead: Do you want to go for a walk?
try: Walk to the kitchen or the mailbox?
strategies.note: Binary choices preserve autonomy while keeping the task manageable.

strategies.title: Shrink the Ask
strategies.description: If something feels too big, make it smaller.
strategies.instead: Let's go for a five-minute walk.
strategies.note: Often once the movement starts, more will follow.

strategies.title: Tie Movement to a Real Activity
strategies.description: Movement feels easier when it has a purpose.
try: Let's walk to the kitchen and get some tea.
Let's go look out the window.
strategies.note: This makes movement feel natural instead of like a chore.

strategies.title: Avoid Emotional Pressure
strategies.description: High emotional intensity can cause the brain to shut down.
strategies.note: Calm communication helps the brain stay engaged.

resistanceIntro: It usually means the **brain is overloaded or tired**, not that they are refusing.
When this happens, the goal is to **reduce pressure and restart gently**.

situations.title: When they say No
avoid: Come on, you need to do this.
try: Okay, how about we just stand up once?
Would you rather do it before lunch or after lunch?

situations.title: When they ignore the request
situations.description: Sometimes the brain needs extra time to process.
try: Let's wait a moment, then say: Let's try standing up once.
Let's walk to the kitchen together.

situations.title: When they become irritated
situations.description: Frustration can happen when the brain feels overwhelmed.
try: Let's grab a glass of water.
situations.note: Switching activities often resets the situation.

situations.title: When they say they are tired
situations.description: Fatigue is very real after stroke.
try: Okay, let's just sit up for a minute.
Let's stand up once and then rest.
situations.note: Reducing the task helps the brain accept it.

situations.title: When they refuse repeatedly
situations.note: Sometimes the brain responds better after a short break.

finalRule.title: Final Rule
lines: Progress happens through patience and repetition.
